retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Prostatitis and Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome in Men -- Treatment / Management -- Specific Therapies for Chronic Prostatitis/Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome (NIH Category III)
retrieved_docs.content: Phosphodiesterase type 5 (PDE-5) inhibitors (eg, sildenafil, tadalafil) and similar therapies can be beneficial for CP/CPPS patients with erectile dysfunction but will not affect other chronic prostatitis symptoms. (See our companion StatPearls reference article on "Erectile Dysfunction." [139] ) Tricyclic antidepressants (eg, amitriptyline, fluoxetine) have shown efficacy in treating neuropathic pain associated with CP/CPPS, in addition to the use of some anticonvulsant-type drugs such as gabapentin and pregabalin. [17] [50] [51] [213] [214] [215] [216] They may also incidentally help treat any associated depression or anxiety issues. Medications without significant therapeutic benefits include allopurinol, leukotriene antagonists (eg, zafirlukast), muscle relaxants (eg, baclofen, diazepam), and pregabalin. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Prostatitis and Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome in Men -- Treatment / Management -- Specific Therapies for Chronic Prostatitis/Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome (NIH Category III)
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding pharmacological pain management, where there is evidence of a nociceptive or inflammatory component, simple analgesics such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatories may offer some benefit. In some patients, continuous use of NSAIDs is beneficial, but early recognition that the pain may be neuropathic and treating it as such with gabapentin, amitryptiline, and duloxetine yields better and more positive outcomes. [18] [218] Pain control in patients with CP/CPPS is critically important and often the most challenging aspect of providing care.

This often involves a combination of treatments, including those listed below. Each therapy listed has at least some evidence of a symptomatic or therapeutic benefit in some CP/CPPS patients, but a combination of treatments is typically necessary for optimal results. retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Prostatitis and Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome in Men -- Treatment / Management -- Specific Therapies for Chronic Prostatitis/Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome (NIH Category III)
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha-blockers relax muscle tension in the prostate and bladder neck, improving urinary flow. [195] They may also be beneficial for long-term pain control. [196] They will be most useful in CP/CPPS patients with urinary symptoms of a weak stream or urinary hesitancy. [197] [198] [199] [200] They can be used empirically, based on symptoms, or after additional testing, such as an automated uroflowmetry and a post-void residual determination. [197] [198] [199] [200] [201] [202] Failure of an α-blocker to relieve urinary flow suggests a urethral stricture or some other form of outflow obstruction, and a retrograde urethrogram or cystoscopy should be considered. Mast cell stabilizers (eg, cromolyn, cetirizine, quercetin, curcumin) may have a role to play in treating patients with CP/CPPS, at least for those who exhibit mast cell dysfunction. [203] [204] [205] Their role in treating prostatitis is still being determined, but a 30-day clinical trial is reasonable.

retrieved_docs.content: Some newly diagnosed CP/CPPS patients will respond to a short (2 to 4-week) clinical trial of antibiotics even without any evidence of infection, but this should not be continued without positive cultures or substantial symptom relief. [134] Patients who've had multiple courses of antibiotics previously are unlikely to respond, which will only increase bacterial resistance.

retrieved_docs.content: Psychological and psychosocial aspects need to be addressed and evaluated as CP/CPPS is worsened by unresolved anxiety, tension, stress, and depression. [219] [223] [224] [225] Patients who exhibit depression, anxiety, suicidal thoughts, or significant relationship problems should be referred for psychological evaluation and treatment. Strategies that address catastrophizing and feelings of helplessness are critical in providing symptom relief as they otherwise will significantly contribute to an increased perception of pain and, ultimately, treatment failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1780
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in benign prostatic hyperplasia are classified as urologicals, a therapeutic category of medications that target disorders of the urinary tract and male reproductive system. As urologicals, these agents are employed to relieve urinary symptoms caused by an enlarged prostate, such as urinary retention, frequency, nocturia, and weak stream, by mechanisms that may include smooth‑muscle relaxation, reduction of prostate volume, or improvement of bladder outflow. Their classification as urologicals underscores their role within the broader pharmacologic management of benign prostatic hyperplasia and related urological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2956
retrieved_docs.content: UROLOGICALS constitute a broad pharmacologic class that includes the specific agents used to treat benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that drugs employed for benign prostatic hypertrophy are a subset of urological medications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2456
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN BENIGN PROSTATIC HYPERTROPHY are classified as a subset of the broader ATC category Other drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy, indicating that these medications fall within this specific therapeutic group for managing benign prostatic hypertrophy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1805
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم) is a central condition that can lead to secondary complications such as chronic kidney disease due to hypertension, indicating that the renal disease is a downstream effect of elevated blood pressure. Clinical observations show that hypertension is frequently associated with symptoms of nausea and vomiting (أعراض الغثيان والقيء) and is linked to broader autonomic nervous system disorders (اضطرابات الجهاز العصبي المستقل), reflecting its systemic impact. Several α‑1 adrenergic antagonists are effective in managing hypertension, including doxazosin, doxazosin‑mesilát, prazosin hydrochloride, terazosin hydrochloride, and terazosin hydrochloride anhydrous, all of which may be used to lower blood pressure. These same agents are also employed in the treatment of benign prostatic hyperplasia (hyperplasia of prostate), providing dual therapeutic benefits for patients who have both elevated blood pressure and prostate enlargement. In the context of benign prostatic hyperplasia, testosterone propionate is contraindicated, indicating that patients receiving this androgen should avoid therapies that could exacerbate prostate growth. Overall, recognizing the interrelationship between hypertension, its autonomic and gastrointestinal manifestations, its potential to cause chronic kidney disease, and the shared pharmacologic options for both hypertension and prostate hyperplasia is essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1352
retrieved_docs.content: The NCI Drug Dictionary Terminology encompasses a broad collection of drug concepts, specifically including acetylcysteine, bismuth subsalicylate, alprostadil, bumetanide, gabapentin, baclofen, inositol, methoxsalen, and rocuronium‑bromide. By incorporating these agents, the terminology provides a standardized reference that spans multiple therapeutic categories—acetylcysteine as a mucolytic, bismuth subsalicylate as an antidiarrheal and gastroprotective agent, alprostadil as a vasodilator used in erectile dysfunction and peripheral vascular disease, bumetanide as a loop diuretic, gabapentin as an anticonvulsant and neuropathic pain medication, baclofen as a muscle relaxant, inositol as a cyclitol involved in cellular signaling, methoxsalen as a psoralen used in photochemotherapy, and rocuronium‑bromide as a non‑depolarizing neuromuscular blocker. This inclusive set supports consistent drug identification, research, and clinical communication across oncology and broader medical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Clidinium bromide is contraindicated in patients who have other diseases of the bladder and urethra, meaning it should not be used when such urological conditions are present. Similarly, propantelin bromide is also contraindicated in the presence of other bladder and urethral diseases, indicating that both medications must be avoided in individuals with these urological disorders to prevent adverse effects or reduced therapeutic benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 221
retrieved_docs.content: Drugs used in benign prostatic hypertrophy (BPH) are classified as urologicals and are organized into two principal subclasses: Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists for BPH, which include silodosin and the fixed‑dose combination dutasteride / tamsulosin, and Testosterone‑5‑alpha reductase inhibitors for BPH, which include finasteride and dutasteride. The dutasteride / tamsulosin oral capsule contains dutasteride 0.5 mg (a 5‑α‑reductase inhibitor) and tamsulosin hydrochloride 0.4 mg (an α‑blocker), providing a coordinated therapeutic approach for BPH. Alpha‑adrenoreceptor antagonists are linked to safety concerns such as poisoning, adverse effects, and risks associated with under‑dosing. Finasteride is also listed under the broader F [Preparations] hierarchy, which encompasses agents such as fentanyl, fospropofol, fenoprofen calcium, ferulic acid, firocoxib, flunarizin‑dihydrochlorid, and fosinoprilat, and may be used to treat prostate tumor (ورم البروستاتا). Antimigraine preparations constitute a separate pharmacologic class indicated for migraine treatment. This class includes clonidine (the only oral‑dose product), other antimigraine preparations catalogued in ATC, the triptan almotriptan, and agents such as pizotylin, atogepant, dichloralphenazone, flunarizin‑dihydrochloride, lisurid, methysergid, and naratriptan. Within antimigraine preparations a subcategory “námelové alkaloidy” identifies drugs derived from a specific alkaloid family. All listed compounds share the clinical purpose of migraine relief despite differing pharmacologic mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1328
retrieved_docs.content: Clidinium and sulfasalazin are both contraindicated in the presence of other diseases of the bladder and urethra, meaning that these medications should not be administered to patients who have any additional bladder or urethral disorders due to safety concerns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1462
retrieved_docs.content: Testosterone propionate is contraindicated in patients with hyperplasia of the prostate, because its androgenic activity can exacerbate prostatic enlargement and worsen the condition.
